key_insight: A Bayesian finite-element model reconstructs 3D vascular velocity and pressure fields from noisy, under-resolved measurements while quantifying uncertainty.
engineering_relevance: This enables clinically relevant flow diagnostics such as pressure drop, flow rate, and wall shear stress estimation without requiring offline training data.
method: Represent velocity and pressure with Taylor-Hood finite element basis functions.
Use maximum-entropy physics-informed priors plus a noise-model likelihood to form a posterior.
Compute the MAP by sparse nonlinear least squares, eliminating pressure analytically and enforcing no-slip walls exactly.
evidence_signal: On patient-specific cerebral aneurysm, aortic aneurysm, and aortic coarctation geometries, the method outperformed tricubic interpolation and matched a PINN for velocity/pressure reconstruction while improving wall shear stress estimates.
watch_item: The approach depends on accurate geometry, mesh/basis design, and a well-specified observation noise model, which may affect robustness in real clinical data.
paper_title: Bayesian finite element regression for vascular flow reconstruction with quantified uncertainty
